metadata.summary: Picking the right agent loop: Plan-Execute for predictable tasks with auditability, ReAct for exploratory goals. Hybrid combines both strengths.

content.title: Plan-Execute vs ReAct — Picking the Right Loop
content.summary: For tasks where the steps are predictable from the goal, use Plan-Execute: one big planning call produces a structured plan, then deterministic code executes it. For tasks where the next step depends on what you just learned, use ReAct: interleave Thought → Action → Observation in a loop. Mixing modes for the wrong job is the #1 cause of agent runs that meander or lock in early.

why.p: Plan-Execute front-loads reasoning. The plan is auditable BEFORE work starts; execution can be parallel; failures are localized to one step. But it cannot adapt — if reality differs from the plan's assumptions, the agent presses on regardless.

ReAct adapts step-by-step. Each Action's Observation feeds the next Thought, so surprising tool outputs reshape the trajectory. But there's no plan to audit; loops can wander, retry, or hit max-turns.
The tradeoff is adaptability vs auditability. Pick by how much you can predict before starting.

list.item: Goal decomposes into known sub-steps → Plan-Execute
Sub-steps are independent (parallelizable) → Plan-Execute
User wants to review the plan before run → Plan-Execute
Each step's input depends on previous output → ReAct
Tool results often surprise (search, web, codebases) → ReAct
Cost-bounded but exploratory → ReAct (with max_turns)
Compliance/audit requirement on intermediate work → Plan-Execute
Long horizon, > 10 steps → Hybrid: plan top-level, ReAct each step
Pure ReAct on a 30-step goal — loop wanders; no human can review trajectory
Pure Plan-Execute on exploration — plan is invented blind; first step's reality breaks the rest
ReAct without max_turns — infinite loops on bad tool output
Plan-Execute that "plans inside execution" — confusing trace; plan changes mid-run
Replan every step — same as ReAct but more expensive

section.name: decision-rule
section.p: Use this table to pick the right mode:

rule.statement: If your task decomposes into 3-10 known sub-steps with fixed order, use Plan-Execute.
rule.rationale: Parallelizability and auditability justify the up-front planning cost.

rule.statement: If each step's success depends on surprising observations from previous steps, use ReAct.
rule.rationale: Adaptability beats auditability; the trajectory emerges from real-time learning.

rule.statement: If your horizon is long (> 10 steps) and mixed predictable/exploratory, use Hybrid.
rule.rationale: Top-level plan is auditable; per-step loops bound exploration.

section.name: hybrid-pattern
section.p: The strongest production pattern is plan + ReAct-per-step:

list.item: Big-model Plan call: produce N structured sub-tasks
For each sub-task, spawn a small ReAct loop (max 5 turns) constrained to that sub-task
Each loop reports a slim result; aggregator updates a state document
Optional Replan call if a sub-task fails or surfaces a contradiction

section.p: This is what Claude Code, LangGraph supervisor+workers, and the deep-agents pattern all converge on.

antipattern.reason: Loop wanders; no human can review trajectory. Add a planning step up front.
Plan is invented blind; first step's reality breaks the rest. Use ReAct or hybrid replan.
Infinite loops on bad tool output. Always cap turns; surface "stuck" as a result.
Confusing trace; plan changes mid-run. Separate the calls: plan first, execute second.
Same as ReAct but more expensive. Replan ONLY on failure or contradiction.

checklist.check: Listed 5-10 representative tasks the agent will face
For each, marked predictable-vs-exploratory
Counted: > 70% predictable → Plan-Execute baseline
> 70% exploratory → ReAct baseline
Mixed → Hybrid (plan top-level, ReAct per step)
Planning prompt produces a STRUCTURED plan (not free text)
Plan schema has goal_restate before plan_steps (reasoning-before-answer)
Each plan step is independently runnable
Plan validation step (sanity check) before any execution
Replan trigger defined (e.g., on step failure)
Always set max_turns (typical: 5-15)
Always set max_total_tokens budget
Tool descriptions written for ReAct context (use-when/NOT-use-when)
Loop emits structured Thought/Action/Observation events for tracing
"Stuck" detection: same action 3x → break with diagnostic
Top-level plan is auditable BEFORE execution starts
Per-step ReAct loops have small max_turns (3-5)
Aggregator builds state document from per-step results
Replan path is explicit (not implicit retry)
On 50 tasks, measured: success rate, avg turns, max turns
No silent infinite loops
Replan firing rate < 10% (otherwise the plan model is too weak)
Auditability: can a human review the plan or trajectory after a failure?

example.title: Plan-Execute fits — code review pipeline
example.description: Goal: review PR #123. Sub-steps known: fetch diff → analyze each file → summarize → post comment.
example.p: Plan-Execute is right: each step is independent, parallelizable, the order is fixed, and the human reviewer can audit the plan before it runs.

example.title: ReAct fits — debugging a flaky test
example.description: Goal: figure out why test X flakes.
example.p: ReAct is right: the next action depends on what stack trace appears. Search → read file → grep → run test → check log. Each tool's output reshapes the next thought.

example.title: Hybrid fits — implement a feature from a spec
example.description: Plan: parse spec → for each requirement (1-N) → write code → write test → run tests → fix on failure → open PR.
example.p: Per-step ReAct: each sub-task has its own loop bounded to 5 turns; if a step's loop maxes out, a replan call decides whether to relax the requirement or escalate. This is the Claude Code default for big tasks.

example.title: Pure ReAct on a 30-step refactor
example.description: A 30-step framework migration done in raw ReAct goes off the rails:

list.item: Loops on the same import 4 times before realizing the dependency renamed
Forgets earlier decisions due to context compaction
No human can review the trajectory because there's no plan to compare against

example.p: Fix: add a Plan call up front producing the migration steps; ReAct each step bounded.

example.title: Plan-Execute for web search
example.description: A research agent given "what is the current Anthropic pricing?" with a strict plan ["search → click first link → extract"] often fails: first link is wrong; extraction returns nothing.
example.p: The right approach is ReAct, which adapts after each search.

example.title: Replan trigger
example.code:
plan: [step1, step2, step3]
step1 succeeds step2 fails with "schema mismatch"
→ trigger replan with state {"step1_done": True, "step2_error": "schema mismatch"} → new plan: [migrate_schema, retry_step2, step3]
example.p: Replan converts a stuck Plan-Execute into Plan-Execute-Plan. Don't replan on every step (that's just ReAct in costume).

example.title: faion-cli hybrid
example.description: Top-level: Plan (one Sonnet call) → 4 SDD tasks. Per-task: ReAct (each task has its own subagent with 8-turn budget). Aggregator: gathers results into state.json. Replan trigger: a failed task's subagent reports confidence: low → orchestrator triggers replan with the failure context.

example.title: Audit trail
example.description: Plan-Execute's auditability is its biggest selling point.
example.p: The plan document is immutable input to execution; it can be reviewed, signed off, and replayed. For regulated work (legal, finance, healthcare), this matters more than adaptability.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.p: Prompt templates for different decision points in the Plan-Execute vs ReAct workflow:
section.prompt:
You operate in a Thought → Action → Observation loop.
After each tool call, observe the result and reflect: - Did this match expectations? - Has the goal been reached? - What's the next concrete step?
If you find yourself repeating the same action with no progress, output `<stuck>` and explain.
You have at most {max_turns} turns. Use them sparingly.

section.name: templates
section.p: Reusable code templates for implementing Plan-Execute, ReAct, and Hybrid patterns:
section.code: def detect_stuck(history: list[dict]) -> bool: last_three_actions = [ msg["content"][0]["name"] + str(msg["content"][0]["input"]) for msg in history[-3:] if msg["role"] == "assistant" and msg["content"][0]["type"] == "tool_use" ] return len(set(last_three_actions)) == 1 # all three identical

from typing import Annotated from pydantic import BaseModel
class Step(BaseModel): description: str tool: str expected_output: str
class Plan(BaseModel): rationale: str steps: Annotated[list[Step], "minimum 1, maximum 10"]

list.item: subagent-as-context-firewall: each sub-task = subagent with isolated context
stream-json-orchestration: orchestrator watches stream events to decide replans
schema-field-order: planning-output schema = [goal_restate, constraints, plan_steps] (reasoning before answer)
